ground_truth.title: FDA's Concerns with Unapproved GLP-1 Drugs Used for Weight Loss
ground_truth.author: Center for Drug Evaluation and Research
ground_truth.main_content: FDA is aware that some patients and health care professionals may look to unapproved versions of GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) receptor agonist drugs, including semaglutide and tirzepatide, as an option for weight loss. This can be risky for patients, as unapproved versions do not undergo FDA's review for safety, effectiveness and quality before they are marketed. A compounded drug might be appropriate if a patient's medical need cannot be met by an FDA-approved drug, or the FDA-approved drug is not commercially available. However, compounded drugs are not FDA approved, meaning the agency does not review them for safety, effectiveness or quality before marketing. The agency has identified concerns for compounded GLP-1 drugs including improper storage during shipping that may lead to quality issues, fraudulent compounded drugs with false labeling, dosing errors resulting in adverse events, and the use of salt forms that are different from approved active ingredients. FDA has received 605 reports of adverse events associated with compounded semaglutide and 545 reports for compounded tirzepatide as of July 31, 2025. The agency urges patients to obtain prescriptions from licensed doctors and fill them at state-licensed pharmacies, and recommends reporting adverse events to MedWatch.
with: FDA is aware that some patients and health care professionals may look to unapproved versions of GLP-1 drugs, including semaglutide and tirzepatide, as an option for weight loss.
Compounded drugs are not FDA approved, meaning the agency does not review them for safety, effectiveness or quality before they are marketed.
FDA has received reports of adverse events related to compounded versions of semaglutide and tirzepatide.
As of July 31, 2025, the FDA has received 605 reports of adverse events associated with compounded semaglutide and 545 reports for compounded tirzepatide.
The agency urges patients to be vigilant when purchasing drugs online and only purchase from state-licensed pharmacies.
without: An official website of the United States government
